source_title: The Best Vitality & Health Protocols | Dr. Rhonda Patrick

paragraphs.speaker: rhonda
paragraphs.name: Dr. Rhonda Patrick
paragraphs.text: There's lots of data now showing that people that are doing these short bursts, at least a minute long, but up to three minutes, they're moving faster with intent. And it's having outsized effects on health outcomes. Individuals that do on the high end, so they're doing three minutes of this short burst of an unstructured type of exercise snack, and they do it three times a day. So it's a total of nine minutes a day. It's associated with a 40% reduction in all-cause mortality, 40% reduction in cancer-related mortality, a 50% reduction in cardiovascular-related mortality.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Thank you for being first person into this public science health education business. You were the first person in, which is why I didn't say first man in, because the first person in was and is a woman. And you've done a marvelous job of educating people on science, how to parse papers and data, health practices.

paragraphs.speaker: rhonda
paragraphs.name: Dr. Rhonda Patrick
paragraphs.text: I was on a professional jump roping team. My friend and I started the team when we were in second grade, and it was called the San Diego Sandskippers. It was part of the International Rope Skipping Organization. Every year we would compete in Boulder, Colorado. I used to get out of school free card, and my partner and I would go and do workshops at other schools and help them start jump rope teams. And the idea was cardiovascular health, healthy heart. I would say, my roots with being a competitive athlete.

paragraphs.speaker: rhonda
paragraphs.name: Dr. Rhonda Patrick
paragraphs.text: For me, exercise is part of my personal hygiene. It really is a non-negotiable. I absolutely have to do exercise, just like I have to brush my teeth. I work out probably about five to six hours a week. I do a lot of high-intensity interval training. I have four days a week where I'm doing at least an hour. Two of those sessions are more of a CrossFit type of training, where I'll do the first 30 minutes will be strength training. So I'll just be lifting heavier with fewer reps. I typically rest about two minutes between my sets. And then the last 30 minutes is more of a high-intensity interval training session.

paragraphs.speaker: rhonda
paragraphs.name: Dr. Rhonda Patrick
paragraphs.text: If I'm traveling or I have an early podcast or something, I'll just jump on the bike, and I have to get that blood flow. Sometimes I'm in my hotel room, and I don't want to go to the gym, I don't have time, and I just, in my room, you know, I do the air squats, I'll do high knees, jumping jacks, and I have to repeat for 10 minutes. There's a variety of brain benefits that have been shown with even just 10 minutes of this vigorous type of intensity of workout. You're immediately increasing neuronal connections. There's been studies showing that you have an improvement in executive function by 14%.

paragraphs.speaker: rhonda
paragraphs.name: Dr. Rhonda Patrick
paragraphs.text: Most of the time with strength training, we start off at five or six and then work our way down. I love deadlifting. I think I'm pretty good at pulling that weight up. Sometimes we'll do six, five, and then we do four twice, and then we do three twice. And then I usually do a drop set after any of those sessions where I'll do 10, and then it's a lot lighter. For me, the strength training is the hardest and there's definitely a mental component where I do not want to do it. It's so unpleasant and you don't want to do it and you do it and it's like that mental toughness that you're building. That's what I experience when I'm doing these strength training exercises.

paragraphs.speaker: rhonda
paragraphs.name: Dr. Rhonda Patrick
paragraphs.text: I've actually been doing a little bit more intermittent fasting. The reason for that is because I noticed that everything that I was doing, I eat healthy, I exercise a lot. And yet, I was sort of gaining more fat in the belly section, the visceral fat. And the only thing that really helped me stop that, put the brakes on, was getting more in a caloric deficit. I personally am on the scale of 1.2 to 1.6 grams per kilogram body weight. I think people should become more obsessed with training and less obsessed with protein. The protein will complement the training.

paragraphs.speaker: rhonda
paragraphs.name: Dr. Rhonda Patrick
paragraphs.text: You want to stop eating three hours before bed. When you're eating food, that's the sympathetic activity. That's activating the sympathetic nervous system. And even as you're digesting. If you're eating right before you go to bed, you are not in that parasympathetic activity part of the cycle that you want to be in.
